text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
END-OF-INDUCTION RESTAGING

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
DOB: [REDACTED]
ORDERING PHYSICIAN: [Oncology Attending]
INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS) with bilobar hepatic metastases. Completion of 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. Restaging to assess treatment response and evaluate for hepatic resection candidacy. Prior imaging for comparison: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 8 weeks prior (after 6 cycles).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of iohexol intravenous contrast. Images were obtained in the portal venous phase through the abdomen and pelvis and in standard contrast-enhanced phase through the chest. Axial images were reconstructed at 2.5 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformations reviewed. No oral contrast was administered. Comparison made to prior CT abdomen/pelvis.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-expanded bilaterally. No focal pulmonary parenchymal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based abnormality is identified. No discrete pulmonary nodules are seen in either lung field. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesion.

Pleura and Pericardium: No pleural effusion. No pericardial effusion. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. The heart is normal in size. There is no hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No anterior mediastinal mass.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures (Thoracic): No acute osseous abnormality. No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions in the thoracic spine, ribs, or sternum. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine, age-appropriate.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contours. There are three discrete hypoattenuating lesions with well-defined margins identified within the hepatic parenchyma; these are markedly diminished from the study performed following cycle 6 of treatment and substantially diminished from the baseline pre-treatment examination.

- Segment VI lesion (right posterior inferior): now measuring approximately 1.6 x 1.3 cm in axial dimension (previously 2.1 x 1.7 cm on the most recent prior study; 3.8 x 3.1 cm at baseline). The lesion appears as a low-density focus with subtle internal heterogeneity, consistent with treated/necrotic metastatic deposit. The margins are more sharply defined than at baseline, reflecting response to therapy. There is no satellite nodularity in the adjacent parenchyma.

- Segment II lesion (left lateral section): now measuring approximately 0.6 x 0.5 cm (unchanged from the prior restaging examination at cycle 6; baseline 1.9 x 1.6 cm). This lesion appears near-isodense with surrounding parenchyma and is only faintly perceptible as a subtle hypoattenuating focus. Its borders are indistinct.

- Segment IV lesion (left medial section): now measuring approximately 0.8 x 0.6 cm (minimally decreased from 1.1 x 0.9 cm on the prior cycle 6 study; baseline 2.2 x 1.8 cm). Discrete hypoattenuating focus with well-demarcated margins. Mild internal homogeneity consistent with treated metastasis.

- Segment VII region: The previously described small hypoattenuating lesion in segment VII is no longer identifiable on the current examination. No discrete lesion or residual enhancement is seen in this region. This lesion appears to have undergone complete radiographic resolution (previously 1.1 x 0.9 cm at baseline, measured 0.5 x 0.4 cm on the cycle 6 study, and now not visualized).

No new hepatic lesions identified. No arterially enhancing hepatic foci suspicious for new metastatic deposits. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The main portal vein is patent and normally enhancing. The hepatic veins are patent. No hepatic vascular thrombus.

Biliary System: No biliary ductal dilation centrally or peripherally. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones.

Pancreas: The pancreas is homogeneous and of normal contour. No ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fat stranding or fluid collection.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with symmetric nephrograms. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass. No nephrolithiasis. A 3 mm simple-appearing cortical cyst is noted in the left kidney, unchanged.

Stomach and Small Bowel: The stomach is normal in appearance. The visualized small bowel loops are of normal caliber and wall thickness, without evidence of obstruction or wall thickening.

Colon and Rectum: There is a focal area of wall thickening and soft tissue density centered within the sigmoid colon measuring approximately 2.4 x 1.9 cm on axial views (previously 3.2 x 2.6 cm on the cycle 6 study; 4.5 x 3.8 cm at baseline). The pericolic fat in this region shows residual inflammatory/desmoplastic stranding, though this is reduced compared with prior examinations. There is no evidence of proximal colonic dilation to suggest high-grade obstruction at this time. No free intraperitoneal air to suggest perforation. The perirectal and presacral regions are unremarkable.

Lymph Nodes (Abdomen): No new enlarged mesenteric, retroperitoneal, or para-aortic lymphadenopathy is identified. The previously described pericolonic lymph nodes in the sigmoid mesentery appear substantially reduced; the largest is now estimated at approximately 7 mm in short axis (previously up to 1.4 cm at baseline). No new pathologic adenopathy.

Peritoneum and Mesentery: No ascites. No peritoneal implants or omental caking identified. The mesentery is otherwise unremarkable.

Aorta and Major Vessels (Abdomen): The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery are patent. No aneurysmal dilatation. Atherosclerotic calcification is minimal.

Abdominal Wall: No abdominal wall hernia.

Osseous Structures (Abdomen/Lumbar): No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions in the lumbar vertebrae or visualized pelvis. Mild degenerative facet changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. No vertebral height loss.

---

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially filled and unremarkable in appearance. No wall thickening or mass.

Prostate: The prostate is slightly enlarged in the expected range for a patient in his late fifties, without a focal suspicious mass. No seminal vesicle abnormality.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Osseous Structures (Pelvis): No suspicious osseous lesion. No focal cortical destruction. No sclerotic or lytic metastases in the iliac wings, acetabula, or proximal femora.

Pelvic Soft Tissues: Unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Ongoing, substantial reduction in hepatic metastatic burden compared with baseline and continued favorable change from the cycle 6 restaging examination. The segment VI lesion (previously the dominant lesion at 3.8 cm) now measures 1.6 x 1.3 cm, representing continued shrinkage. The segment II and segment IV lesions are also markedly diminished or near-stably reduced. The segment VII lesion is no longer visualized on the current study, in keeping with near-complete to complete radiographic response of this deposit.

2. Continued decrease in size of the primary sigmoid colon mass, now approximately 2.4 x 1.9 cm, down from 4.5 x 3.8 cm at baseline and 3.2 x 2.6 cm on the cycle 6 study. The mass continues to narrow but persists. Residual pericolic fat stranding is present, consistent with treated disease and/or desmoplastic reaction.

3. No new hepatic lesions, no peritoneal implants, and no new intra-abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

4. No pulmonary nodules identified on chest CT. No new thoracic metastatic disease.

5. The overall hepatic disease burden, while substantially reduced, includes three residual lesions. The segment II lesion in the left lateral section lies in close proximity to the left hepatic vein; this anatomical relationship warrants careful review by the hepatobiliary surgery team in assessing feasibility of margin-negative resection. The treating team should correlate with multidisciplinary surgical assessment.

6. No adrenal, osseous, or new nodal metastases.

Reported by: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging]
Reviewed with: [Oncology Attending]
